Day 3 · AI Learning Series

Image Prompting: How One Word Can Change Everything

A step-by-step experiment — same scene, four different prompts, four completely different results.

← Back to Hub  /  Day 3: Image Prompting

There's a moment every first-time AI image user goes through. You type something, hit generate, and what comes back looks nothing like what you had in your head. The scene is there, technically. But it feels empty. Flat. Like a stock photo nobody wanted to buy.

That's not the AI failing you. That's the prompt failing the AI.

For this experiment, I picked one scene — a girl sitting in a café on a rainy evening — and refused to change the subject. Same person, same place, same rain. The only thing I changed was how much I told the AI. Here's what happened.

Stage 1 The Bare Minimum
a girl sitting in a café, bright lighting, neutral expression, simple background
5 words no context no mood
What happened: The AI produced something technically correct — yes, there's a girl, yes, she's sitting — but it could be anywhere, any time of day, any mood. The AI had to guess everything, so it defaulted to the average. Bright lighting, neutral expression, forgettable composition. This is what "leaving it up to the AI" actually looks like.
Stage 2 Add the World Around Her
a girl sitting in a café on a rainy evening, warm amber lights inside, dark blue cold light through the window, raindrops on glass
original scene + rain + evening time + contrast lighting
What happened: Just adding weather and time of day shifted everything. The warm-versus-cold contrast — amber inside, blue-grey outside — created a separation between the character and the world. The raindrops on the window gave the image texture and a layer of storytelling. It now looks like somewhere specific, at a specific moment.
Stage 3 Tell It How to Feel
cinematic side portrait of a young woman looking out a rain streaked window, melancholic expression, dramatic lighting, black and white, film photography
previous prompt + emotion (melancholic) + camera angle (side portrait) + cinematic framing + black and white
What happened: Mood words are instructions for expression and posture. "Melancholic" changed the face, the tilt of the head, the way the eyes sit. "Side portrait" moved us from a frontal shot to a profile where the rain-streaked glass becomes part of the story. "Film photography" added the grainy, high-contrast texture that makes it feel like a real photograph, not a render.
Stage 4 The Full Cinematic Brief
cozy cafe scene, ceramic coffee mug with steam, open book on wooden table, rainy window in background, warm golden bokeh, shallow depth of field, 50mm lens, film grain, cinematic color grading
all previous layers + props (mug, book) + 50mm lens / DOF + bokeh + film grain + color grading
What happened: This is the difference between a snapshot and a photograph. The props — ceramic mug, open book — give the scene a life that exists before and after this moment. The photography terms (50mm lens, shallow depth of field, film grain) tell the AI to simulate how a real camera sees this scene. The bokeh turns the rainy window into soft golden circles of light. Combined, it looks like it was shot on a rainy afternoon by someone who waited for exactly this light.

What Each Layer Actually Does

What You Add What It Controls Without It
Environment + time Colour palette, lighting temperature, atmosphere Neutral, flat, timeless in a bad way
Emotion words Facial expression, body posture, energy of the image Blank or generically "happy"
Camera angle Composition — what's in frame, what's cut out Default frontal portrait every time
Props + clothing Storytelling, character context, tactile detail Person exists in a vacuum
Photography terms Depth, focus, grain, colour treatment Looks rendered, not photographed
Lighting contrast Drama, separation, visual depth Everything looks equally lit, equally boring

Why Photography Terms Work So Well

AI image models are trained on billions of real photographs, each tagged with metadata — lens type, aperture, film stock, lighting setup. When you write "50mm lens" or "film grain," you're not being poetic. You're pulling from a specific cluster of images in the model's training data that share a particular look and feel.

"50mm" produces compression and perspective that feels natural to the human eye — the way we actually see. "85mm" gives you a flatter, more isolating portrait look. "24mm wide angle" creates that slightly distorted, immersive feel. These aren't random words — they map to actual photographic behaviour the AI has learned to replicate.

Same with "depth of field" and "bokeh." You're asking the AI to blur everything outside the focal plane, which immediately makes the subject feel deliberate — chosen — rather than just present in the frame.

The Real Lesson

The AI isn't making creative decisions — it's averaging across everything it's seen. A short prompt gets you the average. A detailed prompt pushes it toward a specific corner of that training data where the images look exactly how you described.

That's why adding detail isn't just "more words." Each word is a filter — narrowing down from millions of possibilities to the one image that matches your brief. The more specific you are, the less room there is for the average to creep in.

Start with the bare bones. Add the world. Then the mood. Then the camera. By the time you're done, you're not typing a prompt — you're writing a director's brief.
